methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(vocab_size: int = 30522, hidden_size: int = 768, num_hidden_layers: int = 12, num_attention_heads: int = 12, intermediate_size: int = 3072, hidden_act: TextActivation = 'gelu', max_position_embeddings: int = 512, pad_token_id: int | None = 0, bos_token_id: int | None = None, eos_token_id: int | None = None, hidden_dropout: float = 0.1, attention_dropout: float = 0.1, initializer_range: float = 0.02, layer_norm_eps: float = 1e-12, tie_word_embeddings: bool = True, use_cache: bool = True, type_vocab_size: int = 2, position_embedding_type: Literal['absolute'] = 'absolute', num_labels: int = 2, classifier_dropout: float | None = None)

members.summary: Configuration dataclass for every BERT variant.
members.extended:
Frozen dataclass inheriting all common text-model fields from
`LanguageModelConfig` (`hidden_act`, `hidden_dropout`,
`attention_dropout`, `layer_norm_eps`, `initializer_range`,
`tie_word_embeddings`, ...) and adding the two knobs unique to the BERT
family.  Field names and defaults mirror the reference-framework
`BertConfig` so existing tokenizers and checkpoints map one-to-one onto
Lucid weights.  Defaults match the original `bert-base-uncased` release
(L=12, H=768, A=12, 30,522 WordPiece vocab) from Devlin et al., 2018.

parameters.name: vocab_size
parameters.description:
Size of the WordPiece token vocabulary.  Both BERT-Base and BERT-Large
use 30,522 cased/uncased WordPieces from Devlin et al. 2018.

parameters.name: hidden_size
parameters.description:
Dimensionality $H$ of every transformer hidden state.  Must be
divisible by `num_attention_heads`.

parameters.name: num_hidden_layers
parameters.description: Number $L$ of transformer encoder layers stacked in the trunk.

parameters.name: num_attention_heads
parameters.description:
Number $A$ of parallel attention heads per layer.  Per-head
dimension is `hidden_size // num_attention_heads`.

parameters.name: intermediate_size
parameters.description:
Width of the position-wise feed-forward inner projection (typically
`4 * hidden_size`).

parameters.name: max_position_embeddings
parameters.description:
Maximum sequence length supported by the absolute position-embedding
table.  Inputs longer than this raise a shape error at lookup.

parameters.name: pad_token_id
parameters.description:
Token id treated as padding.  When set, the `Embedding` layer zeroes
out that row (no gradient).

parameters.name: type_vocab_size
parameters.description:
Size of the segment-id vocabulary used by the next-sentence objective.
`2` corresponds to the canonical BERT (segment A / B) setup;
single-segment fine-tunes still use the full table but only ever feed
zeros.

parameters.name: position_embedding_type
parameters.description:
Position encoding flavour.  `"absolute"` is the only one shipped
today — relative-position variants (T5-style, RoFormer-style) live in
their own families.

parameters.name: num_labels
parameters.description:
Number of output classes for downstream classification heads
(`BertForSequenceClassification` / `BertForTokenClassification`).

parameters.name: classifier_dropout
parameters.description:
Optional dropout probability inserted before the task-specific
classifier.  When `None`, the head falls back to `hidden_dropout`.

examples:
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig
>>> cfg = BertConfig()          # BERT-Base defaults
>>> cfg.hidden_size, cfg.num_hidden_layers, cfg.num_attention_heads
(768, 12, 12)
>>> large = BertConfig(
...     hidden_size=1024,
...     num_hidden_layers=24,
...     num_attention_heads=16,
...     intermediate_size=4096,
... )
>>> large.vocab_size
30522
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)).

Validation runs in `__post_init__` and raises `ValueError` for
`type_vocab_size <= 0`, `num_labels <= 0`, or
`classifier_dropout` outside $[0, 1)$.

attributes.name: model_type
attributes.description:
Class-level identifier `"bert"` used by the model registry to look
up the matching factory at load time.

members.name: BertForCausalLM

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(config: BertConfig)

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, token_type_ids: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: BERT trunk repurposed as a left-to-right (causal) language model.
members.extended:
Standard BERT attends bidirectionally; this wrapper injects a
lower-triangular causal mask on top of the existing additive
attention/padding mask so the same encoder weights behave as a decoder.
The LM head is the same tied projection used by
`BertForMaskedLM`.  Use this class when you want to apply
pre-trained BERT weights to a generative or sequence-continuation task.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
BERT hyperparameters.  `config.tie_word_embeddings` (default True)
ties the LM decoder weight to the input embedding matrix.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig, BertForCausalLM
>>> cfg = BertConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = BertForCausalLM(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 2088, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, T=4, V=30522)
(1, 4, 30522)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)); causal adaptation follows the standard left-to-right
LM masking scheme.

The additive causal mask $M \in \mathbb{R}^{T \times T}$ satisfies

$$
M_{ij} =
\begin{cases}
    0, & j \le i \\
    -10^{4}, & j > i
\end{cases}
$$
and is broadcast against a padding mask when present.  Loss (when
`labels` is supplied) uses the standard next-token shift:

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{CLM}}
    = -\frac{1}{B(T-1)} \sum_{b,t}
      \log p_{\theta}(y_{b, t+1} \mid x_{b,\le t}),
$$
with positions labelled `-100` excluded.

attributes.name: bert
attributes.description:
Underlying transformer trunk; only `embeddings` and `encoder` are
invoked in `forward` (the pooler is bypassed).

attributes.name: cls
attributes.description:
Tied LM prediction head — same architecture as
`BertForMaskedLM`.

methods.signature: __init__(config: BertConfig)
methods.summary: Initialise the module and validate the supplied config.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Must be an instance of the subclass's declared
`config_class`.

raises.description:
If `config_class` has not been set on the concrete subclass,
or if `config` is not an instance of `config_class`.

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, token_type_ids: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: BERT with a tied masked-language-modeling head.
members.extended:
Implements the masked-LM half of the [Devlin et al. (2018)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805) pre-training
objective.  A two-layer projection (dense + GELU + LayerNorm) maps each
hidden state to vocabulary logits via a decoder whose weight matrix is
tied to the input `word_embeddings` table when
`config.tie_word_embeddings` is True.  Use for pre-training from
scratch, continued pre-training on domain corpora, or fill-in-the-blank
inference.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
BERT hyperparameters.  `config.tie_word_embeddings` (default True)
controls whether the decoder weight is bound to the input embedding
matrix to halve the parameter count.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig, BertForMaskedLM
>>> cfg = BertConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = BertForMaskedLM(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 103, 102]])   # [CLS] hello [MASK] [SEP]
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, T=4, V=30522)
(1, 4, 30522)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)), section 3.1 Task #1.

When `labels` is supplied the head computes

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{MLM}} = -\frac{1}{|M|}
    \sum_{i \in M} \log p_{\theta}(x_i \mid x_{\setminus M})
$$
over the set $M$ of masked positions, with positions where the
label equals `-100` excluded from the sum.

attributes.name: bert
attributes.description: Underlying bidirectional encoder trunk.

attributes.name: cls
attributes.description:
Masked-LM prediction head with its own dense + LayerNorm transform
and an output decoder of shape `(hidden_size, vocab_size)`.

members.name: BertForNextSentencePrediction

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(config: BertConfig)

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, token_type_ids: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: BERT with the standalone next-sentence-prediction head.
members.extended:
Wraps the bidirectional encoder with a single binary linear classifier
operating on the pooled `[CLS]` embedding.  This is pre-training task 2
of Devlin et al., 2018 §3.1 in isolation — useful for reproducing
historical experiments or as a sanity check for sentence-pair coherence.

NSP was abandoned by RoBERTa, ALBERT, and DeBERTa as offering no
downstream value, so prefer `BertForMaskedLM` (MLM-only) or
`BertForSequenceClassification` for new work.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description: BERT hyperparameters.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig, BertForNextSentencePrediction
>>> cfg = BertConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = BertForNextSentencePrediction(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102, 2088, 102]])
>>> token_type_ids = lucid.tensor([[0, 0, 0, 1, 1]])
>>> out = model(input_ids, token_type_ids=token_type_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, 2)
(1, 2)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)) §3.1 (task #2).

When `labels` is provided, the loss is the binary cross-entropy

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{NSP}}
    = -\frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B}
      \log p_{\theta}(y_b \mid x_b^{(A)}, x_b^{(B)}),
$$
where $y_b \in \{0, 1\}$ denotes IsNext vs. NotNext.

attributes.name: bert
attributes.description: Underlying bidirectional encoder trunk.

attributes.name: cls
attributes.description:
NSP head — a single `Linear(hidden_size, 2)` over the pooled
embedding.

members.name: BertForPreTraining

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(config: BertConfig)

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, token_type_ids: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None, next_sentence_label: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: BERT with the original joint MLM + NSP pre-training objective.
members.extended:
Combines the masked-language-modeling head (decoder weight tied to input
embeddings when `config.tie_word_embeddings` is True) with the
next-sentence-prediction head on top of the pooled `[CLS]` embedding.
This is the exact head configuration used in Devlin et al., 2018 to
train BERT-Base and BERT-Large from scratch.

Supply `labels` (MLM targets) and/or `next_sentence_label` (binary
NSP target) to compute the corresponding losses; their sum is exposed as
`output.loss`.  Use this class only when reproducing the original
pre-training recipe — newer encoder-only LMs typically drop NSP and use
`BertForMaskedLM` directly.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
BERT hyperparameters.  `config.tie_word_embeddings` (default True)
controls whether the MLM decoder weight is tied to the input
embedding matrix.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig, BertForPreTraining
>>> cfg = BertConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = BertForPreTraining(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 103, 102, 2088, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.prediction_logits.shape    # MLM logits  (B=1, T=6, V=30522)
(1, 6, 30522)
>>> out.seq_relationship_logits.shape   # NSP logits  (B=1, 2)
(1, 2)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)) §3.1.

The combined loss when both objectives are supplied is

$$
\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{pretrain}}
    = \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{MLM}} + \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{NSP}}.
$$

attributes.name: bert
attributes.description: Underlying bidirectional encoder trunk.

attributes.name: cls
attributes.description:
Combined head holding both the MLM prediction projection
(`cls.predictions`) and the NSP binary linear
(`cls.seq_relationship`).

members.name: BertForPreTrainingOutput
members.signature: BertForPreTrainingOutput(prediction_logits: Tensor, seq_relationship_logits: Tensor, loss: Tensor | None = None, mlm_loss: Tensor | None = None, nsp_loss: Tensor | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(prediction_logits: Tensor, seq_relationship_logits: Tensor, loss: Tensor | None = None, mlm_loss: Tensor | None = None, nsp_loss: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: Combined output for `BertForPreTraining`.
members.extended:
Aggregates the masked-LM logits, next-sentence-prediction logits, and
optional per-objective and combined losses produced by the full BERT
pre-training pipeline of Devlin et al., 2018.

parameters.name: prediction_logits
parameters.description:
MLM head logits of shape `(B, T, vocab_size)` — one distribution
over the WordPiece vocabulary per input position.

parameters.name: seq_relationship_logits
parameters.description:
NSP head logits of shape `(B, 2)` — binary IsNext / NotNext scores
derived from the pooled `[CLS]` embedding.

parameters.name: loss
mlm_loss
parameters.description: Cross-entropy on masked positions when `labels` was supplied.

parameters.name: nsp_loss
parameters.description:
Binary cross-entropy on the NSP head when `next_sentence_label`
was supplied.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig, BertForPreTraining
>>> cfg = BertConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = BertForPreTraining(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102, 2088, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.prediction_logits.shape, out.seq_relationship_logits.shape
((1, 5, 30522), (1, 2))
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)) §3.1.

members.name: BertForQuestionAnswering

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(config: BertConfig)

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, token_type_ids: Tensor | None = None, start_positions: Tensor | None = None, end_positions: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: BERT with a 2-way span head for extractive question answering.
members.extended:
Wraps the bidirectional encoder with a single linear of output width 2,
producing start- and end-position logits over each token in the input.
This is the SQuAD v1.1 / v2.0 fine-tuning recipe of Devlin et al., 2018
§4.2 — given a `(question, context)` pair concatenated with `[SEP]`,
the model predicts the answer span inside the context.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
BERT hyperparameters.  The QA head is always 2-way; `num_labels`
is ignored here.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig, BertForQuestionAnswering
>>> cfg = BertConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = BertForQuestionAnswering(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 2040, 2003, 102, 1045, 2572, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, T=7, 2)
(1, 7, 2)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)).

When both `start_positions` and `end_positions` are provided, the
loss is the symmetric average of two cross-entropies:

$$
\mathcal{L} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(
    \mathrm{CE}(z^{\mathrm{start}}, y^{\mathrm{start}})
  + \mathrm{CE}(z^{\mathrm{end}},   y^{\mathrm{end}})
\right).
$$
The returned `logits` tensor has shape `(B, T, 2)`; index `[..., 0]`
for start scores and `[..., 1]` for end scores.

attributes.name: bert
attributes.description: Underlying bidirectional encoder trunk.

attributes.name: qa_outputs
attributes.description:
Final linear of shape `(hidden_size, 2)` mapping each token's
hidden state to `(start_logit, end_logit)`.

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(config: BertConfig)

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, token_type_ids: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None)

parameters.name: input_ids
parameters.description: `(B, S)` source token ids.

parameters.name: decoder_input_ids
parameters.description: `(B, T)` target token ids (right-shifted).

parameters.name: attention_mask
parameters.description: Optional `(B, S)` HF-style padding mask.

parameters.name: decoder_attention_mask
parameters.description: Optional `(B, T)` HF-style padding mask.

returns.description: class:`Seq2SeqLMOutput` carrying decoder hidden states as

members.summary: BERT with a pooled-CLS linear classifier for sequence-level tasks.
members.extended:
Wraps the bidirectional encoder with a dropout-regularised linear head
operating on the `[CLS]` pooled embedding.  This is the standard
fine-tuning recipe for GLUE-style sentence/sentence-pair tasks (SST-2,
MNLI, QQP, RTE, ...) introduced in Devlin et al., 2018 §4.1.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
BERT hyperparameters.  `config.num_labels` sets the output
dimension; `config.classifier_dropout` (falling back to
`hidden_dropout`) sets the dropout applied before the linear.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig, BertForSequenceClassification
>>> cfg = BertConfig(num_labels=3, num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = BertForSequenceClassification(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, num_labels=3)
(1, 3)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)).

The pooled representation is
$p = \tanh(W_{\mathrm{pool}}\, h_{[\mathrm{CLS}]} + b_{\mathrm{pool}})$,
and the final logits are $z = W_{\mathrm{cls}}\,\mathrm{Dropout}(p) + b_{\mathrm{cls}}$.
When `labels` is provided, cross-entropy over `num_labels` classes is
computed and exposed as `output.loss`.

attributes.name: bert
attributes.description: Underlying bidirectional encoder trunk.

attributes.name: dropout
attributes.description: Dropout layer applied to the pooled `[CLS]` embedding.

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description: Final linear of shape `(hidden_size, num_labels)` producing logits.

members.name: BertForTokenClassification

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(config: BertConfig)

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, token_type_ids: Tensor | None = None, labels: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: BERT with a per-token linear classifier for tagging tasks.
members.extended:
Wraps the bidirectional encoder with a dropout-regularised linear head
applied independently at every sequence position.  Used for token-level
fine-tunes such as named-entity recognition (CoNLL-2003), part-of-speech
tagging, and chunking — see Devlin et al., 2018 §4.3.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
BERT hyperparameters.  `config.num_labels` sets the per-position
output dimension; `config.classifier_dropout` (falling back to
`hidden_dropout`) sets the dropout applied before the linear.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig, BertForTokenClassification
>>> cfg = BertConfig(num_labels=9, num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128,
...                  num_attention_heads=2, intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = BertForTokenClassification(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 2088, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (B=1, T=4, num_labels=9)
(1, 4, 9)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)).

The loss (when `labels` is provided) is the masked cross-entropy
inherited from `MaskedLMMixin`:

$$
\mathcal{L} = -\frac{1}{|V|}
    \sum_{(b, t) \in V} \log p_{\theta}\!\left(y_{b,t} \mid x_b\right),
$$
where $V$ is the set of positions with `label != -100`.

attributes.name: bert
attributes.description: Underlying bidirectional encoder trunk.

attributes.name: dropout
attributes.description: Dropout applied to the full sequence hidden states.

attributes.name: classifier
attributes.description:
Final linear of shape `(hidden_size, num_labels)` mapping each
token's hidden state to per-class logits.

members.name: BertModel

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(config: BertConfig)

methods.name: get_input_embeddings
methods.signature: get_input_embeddings()

methods.name: set_input_embeddings
methods.signature: set_input_embeddings(value: nn.Module)

methods.name: forward
methods.signature: forward(input_ids: Tensor, attention_mask: Tensor | None = None, token_type_ids: Tensor | None = None)

members.summary: Bare BERT encoder returning hidden states and pooled CLS embedding.
members.extended:
Implements the bidirectional transformer encoder of Devlin et al., 2018.
Token, position, and segment embeddings are summed, LayerNormed, and
dropout-regularised, then passed through $L$ transformer blocks of
multi-head self-attention plus position-wise feed-forward.  A single
tanh-activated linear ("pooler") on the first `[CLS]` token produces a
sentence-level embedding used by classification heads.

Use this class as the trunk when you want raw hidden states; the
task-specific subclasses (`BertFor*`) wrap it with appropriate heads.

parameters.name: config
parameters.description:
Hyperparameters controlling vocabulary, depth, width, head count, and
regularisation.  See `BertConfig` for the full field list.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertConfig, BertModel
>>> cfg = BertConfig(num_hidden_layers=2, hidden_size=128, num_attention_heads=2,
...                  intermediate_size=512)
>>> model = BertModel(cfg).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 2088, 102]])   # [CLS] hello world [SEP]
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (B=1, T=4, H=128)
(1, 4, 128)
>>> out.pooler_output.shape       # (B=1, H=128)
(1, 128)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)).

Self-attention follows the scaled dot-product form

$$
\mathrm{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \mathrm{softmax}\!\left(
    \frac{Q K^{\top}}{\sqrt{d_k}}
\right) V
$$
with $d_k = H / A$.  Each layer applies multi-head attention,
followed by a feed-forward block

$$
\mathrm{FFN}(x) = \mathrm{GELU}(x W_1 + b_1) W_2 + b_2,
$$
each wrapped by a residual connection and post-LayerNorm.

attributes.name: embeddings
attributes.description:
Token + position + token-type embedding block followed by LayerNorm
and dropout.

attributes.name: encoder
attributes.description: Stack of `config.num_hidden_layers` transformer encoder layers.

attributes.name: pooler
attributes.description: Dense + tanh projection of the `[CLS]` hidden state.

attributes.name: config_class
attributes.description:
Class-level pointer used by the registry to instantiate a matching
config from disk.

attributes.name: base_model_prefix
attributes.description:
Prefix (`"bert"`) under which sub-module checkpoints are nested in
task-head variants — used during weight loading.

members.name: BertTokenizer
members.signature: BertTokenizer(vocab: dict[str, int], unk_token: str = '[UNK]', continuing_prefix: str = '##', max_chars_per_word: int = 100, do_lower_case: bool = True, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, pre_tokenizer: PreTokenizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(vocab: dict[str, int], unk_token: str = '[UNK]', continuing_prefix: str = '##', max_chars_per_word: int = 100, do_lower_case: bool = True, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, pre_tokenizer: PreTokenizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

members.summary: BERT tokenizer — pure-Python reference.
members.extended:
A thin convenience subclass of
`lucid.utils.tokenizer.WordPieceTokenizer` with BERT's
canonical `[UNK]/[CLS]/[SEP]/[PAD]/[MASK]` registry, the
`lucid.utils.tokenizer.normalizers.BertNormalizer`
(lowercase + accent-strip + Chinese-char split + clean-text), and
`lucid.utils.tokenizer.pre_tokenizers.WhitespacePunctuationSplit`
pre-tokenizer baked in as defaults.

Loads any published HF BERT-family `vocab.txt` checkpoint
unchanged.

parameters.name: vocab
parameters.description:
BERT-style vocab.  Continuation subwords prefixed with `##`.
Pass `{}` and call `WordPieceTokenizer.train` to
train a fresh vocab from a corpus.

parameters.name: unk_token
parameters.description:
Token emitted when no valid longest-match prefix is found.
Must be present in `vocab` for the id to materialise.

parameters.name: continuing_prefix
parameters.description: Marker for non-initial subwords (BERT convention).

parameters.name: max_chars_per_word
parameters.description:
Words longer than this skip the longest-match loop and emit
`unk_token` directly (mirrors BERT `BasicTokenizer`).

parameters.name: do_lower_case
parameters.description:
Forwarded to `BertNormalizer`.  Set to `False` for
cased checkpoints (`bert-base-cased`, multilingual models).
Ignored if `normalizer` is given explicitly.

parameters.name: normalizer
parameters.description: Override the default `BertNormalizer`.

parameters.name: pre_tokenizer
parameters.description: Override the default `WhitespacePunctuationSplit`.

parameters.name: special_tokens
parameters.description: Override the default BERT registry.

examples:
Train a small BERT-style tokenizer from a corpus, then round-trip:
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import BertTokenizer
>>> tok = BertTokenizer(vocab={})
>>> tok.train(["hello world", "hello bert"] * 8, vocab_size=60)
>>> ids = tok.encode("Hello World", add_special_tokens=False)
>>> # default do_lower_case=True → "Hello" and "hello" share ids.
>>> tok.decode(ids, skip_special_tokens=False)
'hello world'

members.name: BertTokenizerFast
members.signature: BertTokenizerFast(vocab: dict[str, int], unk_token: str = '[UNK]', continuing_prefix: str = '##', max_chars_per_word: int = 100, do_lower_case: bool = True, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, pre_tokenizer: PreTokenizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

methods.name: __init__
methods.signature: __init__(vocab: dict[str, int], unk_token: str = '[UNK]', continuing_prefix: str = '##', max_chars_per_word: int = 100, do_lower_case: bool = True, normalizer: Normalizer | None = None, pre_tokenizer: PreTokenizer | None = None, special_tokens: SpecialTokens | None = None)

members.summary: BERT tokenizer — C++-backed.
members.extended:
Bit-identical to `BertTokenizer`: same vocab format, same
special-token registry, same encode output.  The greedy
longest-match loop runs in C++ via
`lucid._C.engine.utils.tokenizer.WordPiece`, typically
20-30× faster than the pure-Python flavour on large corpora.

Constructor parameters mirror `BertTokenizer` exactly —
see that class for the full reference.

members.name: bert_base
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Base encoder.
members.extended:
Canonical mid-size variant from Devlin et al., 2018 Table 1:
$L=12$ transformer layers, $H=768$ hidden, $A=12$
attention heads, intermediate width 3072 — roughly 110M parameters.
Uses the 30,522-piece WordPiece vocabulary and a maximum sequence length
of 512.  This is the default starting point for most BERT fine-tuning
work.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future weight registration.  Returns a randomly
initialised model today.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides (e.g. `vocab_size=...`,
`max_position_embeddings=...`) forwarded into the underlying
config.

returns.description: Encoder trunk configured with the BERT-Base size and any overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_base
>>> model = bert_base().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 2088, 102]])   # [CLS] hello world [SEP]
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape, out.pooler_output.shape
((1, 4, 768), (1, 768))
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)), Table 1 (BERT\ :sub:`BASE`).

members.name: bert_base_cls
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Base model with a sequence-classification head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `bert_base` (L=12, H=768, A=12), augmented with a
pooled-CLS linear classifier of output width `config.num_labels`.  The
canonical fine-tuning recipe for GLUE-style single-sentence and
sentence-pair tasks (SST-2, MNLI, QQP, RTE, MRPC, STS-B).

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.  Pass `num_labels=N` to set the number of
classes (default 2).

returns.description: BERT-Base wrapped with the pooled classifier head.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_base_cls
>>> model = bert_base_cls(num_labels=3).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, num_labels=3)
(1, 3)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)) §4.1.

members.name: bert_base_mlm
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Base model with a tied masked-LM head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `bert_base` (L=12, H=768, A=12, ~110M parameters),
augmented with the masked-language-modeling prediction head used at
pre-training time.  Suitable for continued pre-training on domain
corpora, mask-filling inference, and reproducing the MLM half of the
[Devlin et al. (2018)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805) pre-training recipe.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: BERT-Base wrapped with the tied MLM head.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_base_mlm
>>> model = bert_base_mlm().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 103, 102]])   # [CLS] hello [MASK] [SEP]
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, 4, vocab_size=30522)
(1, 4, 30522)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)) §3.1 (task #1).

members.name: bert_base_qa
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Base model with an extractive-QA span head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `bert_base` (L=12, H=768, A=12), augmented with a
2-way linear head producing start- and end-position logits over each
input token.  The canonical fine-tuning recipe for SQuAD v1.1 and v2.0.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.  `num_labels` is ignored by this head.

returns.description: BERT-Base wrapped with the span-prediction head.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_base_qa
>>> model = bert_base_qa().eval()
>>> # question + context (CLS q [SEP] c [SEP]) ids
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 2040, 102, 1045, 2572, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, T=6, 2) — last dim is (start, end)
(1, 6, 2)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)) §4.2.

members.name: bert_base_token_cls
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Base model with a per-token classification head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `bert_base` (L=12, H=768, A=12), augmented with a
per-position linear classifier of output width `config.num_labels`.
The canonical fine-tuning recipe for sequence-labelling tasks: named-
entity recognition (CoNLL-2003), part-of-speech tagging, chunking.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.  Pass `num_labels=N` to set the tag set size.

returns.description: BERT-Base wrapped with the per-token classifier head.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_base_token_cls
>>> model = bert_base_token_cls(num_labels=9).eval()   # e.g. CoNLL-2003 BIO tag set
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 2198, 7592, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, T=4, num_labels=9)
(1, 4, 9)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)) §4.3.

members.name: bert_large
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Large encoder.
members.extended:
Larger of the two original variants from Devlin et al., 2018 Table 1:
$L=24$ transformer layers, $H=1024$ hidden, $A=16$
attention heads, intermediate width 4096 — roughly 340M parameters.
Uses the 30,522-piece WordPiece vocabulary and a maximum sequence length
of 512.  Achieved SOTA on every GLUE task and SQuAD v1.1/v2.0 at release.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future weight registration.  Returns a randomly
initialised model today.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: Encoder trunk configured with the BERT-Large size and any overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_large
>>> model = bert_large().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 2088, 102]])   # [CLS] hello world [SEP]
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape, out.pooler_output.shape
((1, 4, 1024), (1, 1024))
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)), Table 1 (BERT\ :sub:`LARGE`).

members.name: bert_large_cls
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Large model with a sequence-classification head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `bert_large` (L=24, H=1024, A=16, ~340M parameters),
augmented with a pooled-CLS linear classifier of output width
`config.num_labels`.  Use for high-accuracy GLUE-style fine-tunes when
compute permits.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.  Pass `num_labels=N` to set the number of
classes (default 2).

returns.description: BERT-Large wrapped with the pooled classifier head.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_large_cls
>>> model = bert_large_cls(num_labels=2).eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, 2)
(1, 2)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)) §4.1.

members.name: bert_large_mlm
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Large model with a tied masked-LM head.
members.extended:
Same trunk as `bert_large` (L=24, H=1024, A=16, ~340M parameters),
augmented with the masked-language-modeling prediction head used at
pre-training time.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: BERT-Large wrapped with the tied MLM head.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_large_mlm
>>> model = bert_large_mlm().eval()
>>> input_ids = lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 103, 102]])
>>> out = model(input_ids)
>>> out.logits.shape   # (1, 4, 30522)
(1, 4, 30522)
notes:
Reference: Devlin, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"BERT: Pre-training of
Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding"*, NAACL 2019
([arXiv:1810.04805](https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.04805)) §3.1 (task #1).

members.name: bert_medium
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Medium encoder.
members.extended:
Largest of the Turc et al., 2019 distillation sizes: $L=8$ layers,
$H=512$ hidden, $A=8$ heads, intermediate width 2048.
Roughly 41M parameters — closest in depth to Base while keeping the
Small width.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: Encoder trunk configured with the BERT-Medium size and any overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_medium
>>> model = bert_medium().eval()
>>> out = model(lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102]]))
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (1, 3, 512)
(1, 3, 512)
notes:
Reference: Turc, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"Well-Read Students Learn
Better"*, 2019 ([arXiv:1908.08962](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.08962)).

members.name: bert_mini
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Mini encoder.
members.extended:
Second-smallest of the Turc et al., 2019 distillation sizes:
$L=4$ layers, $H=256$ hidden, $A=4$ heads, intermediate
width 1024.  Roughly 11M parameters.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: Encoder trunk configured with the BERT-Mini size and any overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_mini
>>> model = bert_mini().eval()
>>> out = model(lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102]]))
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (1, 3, 256)
(1, 3, 256)
notes:
Reference: Turc, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"Well-Read Students Learn
Better"*, 2019 ([arXiv:1908.08962](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.08962)).

members.name: bert_small
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Small encoder.
members.extended:
Distillation-targeted variant from Turc et al., 2019: $L=4$ layers,
$H=512$ hidden, $A=8$ heads, intermediate width 2048.
Roughly 29M parameters — a useful mid-point between Mini and Base.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description: Reserved for future weight registration; currently a no-op.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides forwarded into the
underlying config.

returns.description: Encoder trunk configured with the BERT-Small size and any overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_small
>>> model = bert_small().eval()
>>> out = model(lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102]]))
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (1, 3, 512)
(1, 3, 512)
notes:
Reference: Turc, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"Well-Read Students Learn
Better"*, 2019 ([arXiv:1908.08962](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.08962)).

members.name: bert_tiny
members.summary: Construct a BERT-Tiny encoder.
members.extended:
Smallest of the four distillation-targeted variants from Turc, Chang,
Lee, and Toutanova, *"Well-Read Students Learn Better"*, 2019
([arXiv:1908.08962](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.08962)): $L=2$ transformer layers, $H=128$ hidden,
$A=2$ heads, intermediate width 512.  Roughly 4M parameters — use
for CPU/edge inference, latency-bound demos, or unit tests of the BERT
pipeline.

parameters.name: pretrained
parameters.description:
Reserved for future weight registration.  No checkpoints are wired up
yet, so the model is always returned with random initialisation.

parameters.name: **overrides
parameters.description:
Optional `BertConfig` field overrides (e.g. `vocab_size=...`,
`num_labels=...`).  Forwarded into a fresh `BertConfig` whose
defaults match the Tiny size table above.

returns.description: Encoder trunk configured with the BERT-Tiny size and any overrides.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.models.text.bert import bert_tiny
>>> model = bert_tiny().eval()
>>> out = model(lucid.tensor([[101, 7592, 102]]))
>>> out.last_hidden_state.shape   # (1, 3, 128)
(1, 3, 128)
notes:
Reference: Turc, Chang, Lee, and Toutanova, *"Well-Read Students Learn
Better: On the Importance of Pre-training Compact Models"*, 2019
([arXiv:1908.08962](https://arxiv.org/abs/1908.08962)).

summary: BERT family — Devlin et al., 2018 (encoder-only transformer).
citation: Devlin, Jacob, et al. "BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding." Proceedings of NAACL-HLT, 2019, pp. 4171–4186.
theory:
BERT — *Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers* —
is a transformer **encoder** pre-trained on two unsupervised
objectives over raw text:

1. **Masked Language Modelling (MLM).**  A random 15% of input
   tokens are replaced with a ``[MASK]`` placeholder; the model
   must predict the original token from its bidirectional
   context.  Formally, for masked positions $\mathcal{M}$,



$$
\mathcal{L}_{\text{MLM}}
    = -\,\mathbb{E}_{x}\!\left[\;
        \sum_{i \in \mathcal{M}}
            \log p_\theta\!\bigl(x_i \mid x_{\setminus \mathcal{M}}\bigr)
    \right].
$$

   Unlike the left-to-right LMs that preceded it, every token's
   prediction conditions on **both** left and right context — hence
   *bidirectional*.

2. **Next-Sentence Prediction (NSP).**  Two segments $A$ and
   $B$ are concatenated with separator tokens; the model
   reads the pooled ``[CLS]`` representation and predicts whether
   $B$ was the actual next sentence in the corpus or a
   random other sentence.  This objective gives the model a
   coarse notion of inter-sentence coherence used by downstream
   sentence-pair tasks (QA, NLI).

After pre-training on BookCorpus + English Wikipedia, the same
encoder is fine-tuned on individual downstream tasks by adding a
thin task-specific head on top of either the ``[CLS]`` token
representation (classification) or all per-token representations
(tagging, span prediction).  This "pre-train once, fine-tune
everywhere" recipe defined the post-2018 NLP landscape and set
new state-of-the-art on the GLUE, SQuAD, and SWAG benchmarks at
publication time.
